claims.claim_text: KubeArmor is a cloud-native runtime security enforcement system that restricts process, file, and network behavior for pods, containers, and nodes, using LSMs plus eBPF-based telemetry.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: The KubeArmor upstream README directly describes runtime enforcement scope, LSM backends, and eBPF-generated alerts.

claims.claim_text: Admission controllers intercept API requests before persistence, while KubeArmor constrains behavior after the pod is running.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters; section: 5. Choosing KubeArmor Alongside Falco, Tetragon, and Kubernetes Controls
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes admission-controller reference covers the pre-persistence boundary, and KubeArmor's FAQ explicitly contrasts that with runtime protection.

claims.claim_text: KubeArmor defines three main policy types: KubeArmorPolicy for workloads, KubeArmorHostPolicy for hosts/nodes, and KubeArmorClusterPolicy for cluster-wide workload targeting.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. KubeArmor Architecture and Enforcement Boundaries; component table
claims.rationale: The official KubeArmor docs page directly enumerates KSP, HSP, and CSP, but the host is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: KubeArmor workload policies select pods by labels and can match process, file, network, capabilities, and syscall behavior.
claims.span_hint: section: 3. Designing KubeArmorPolicy From Behavior, Not Syntax
claims.rationale: The upstream policy specification directly documents selector semantics and the supported policy sections.

claims.claim_text: KubeArmor supports Allow, Audit, and Block actions, and Audit is intended for verification before applying Block.
claims.span_hint: section: 3. Designing KubeArmorPolicy From Behavior, Not Syntax; section: 4. Rollout Strategy: Visibility, Audit, Enforce, Operate
claims.rationale: The upstream spec explicitly documents the three actions and the verification role of Audit.

claims.claim_text: `karmor probe` is a primary support check, and a blank `Active LSM` indicates that enforcement will not work.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. KubeArmor Architecture and Enforcement Boundaries
claims.rationale: The upstream FAQ directly says to use `karmor probe` for support checks and explains the meaning of a blank `Active LSM`.

claims.claim_text: If a platform lacks a supported enforcement mechanism, Block policies may still yield observability data even though enforcement is not observed.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. KubeArmor Architecture and Enforcement Boundaries; backend table
claims.rationale: The upstream FAQ directly describes observability continuing even when blocking cannot be enforced.

claims.claim_text: Unless automounting is disabled, the ServiceAccount admission controller adds a projected API-token volume and mounts it at `/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount` in Linux containers.
claims.span_hint: section: 3. Designing KubeArmorPolicy From Behavior, Not Syntax; section: 4. Rollout Strategy: Visibility, Audit, Enforce, Operate; section: 6. Worked Incident
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes ServiceAccount admin reference directly documents the projected token volume and mount path.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes NetworkPolicy is an application-centric L3/L4 control that governs pod communication using pod, namespace, and IP selectors.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Choosing KubeArmor Alongside Falco, Tetragon, and Kubernetes Controls; comparison table
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes NetworkPolicy concept page directly defines its L3/L4 scope and selector model.

claims.claim_text: KubeArmor network rules use `matchProtocols` to match protocol families rather than pod-to-pod or namespace-to-namespace connectivity.
claims.span_hint: section: 1. The Security Model Problem KubeArmor Solves; section: 5. Choosing KubeArmor Alongside Falco, Tetragon, and Kubernetes Controls
claims.rationale: The upstream policy specification shows that KubeArmor network matching is expressed through protocol-family rules.

claims.claim_text: KubeArmor can express a `network.matchProtocols` rule for `raw` and use it to block raw socket behavior.
claims.span_hint: section: 1. The Security Model Problem KubeArmor Solves; section: 4. Rollout Strategy: Visibility, Audit, Enforce, Operate; section: 6. Worked Incident
claims.rationale: The official KubeArmor blog shows a concrete `protocol: raw` example, but the host is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: AppArmor is common on Ubuntu and Debian nodes.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. KubeArmor Architecture and Enforcement Boundaries; backend table
claims.anchor_text: | AppArmor | Common on Ubuntu and Debian nodes | Mature profile-based enforcement for process and file behavior | Requires AppArmor support and profile loading on the node |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | AppArmor | Common on AppArmor-enabled Linux nodes | Mature profile-based enforcement for process and file behavior | Requires AppArmor support and profile loading on the node |
claims.rationale: The allowlisted sources support AppArmor as a KubeArmor backend, but not this exact distro mapping.

claims.claim_text: SELinux is common on RHEL, Fedora, and enterprise Linux nodes.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. KubeArmor Architecture and Enforcement Boundaries; backend table
claims.anchor_text: | SELinux | Common on RHEL, Fedora, and enterprise Linux nodes | Strong label-based enforcement model | Existing SELinux policy posture may affect expectations |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | SELinux | Common on SELinux-enabled Linux nodes | Strong label-based enforcement model | Existing SELinux policy posture may affect expectations |
claims.rationale: The verified sources support SELinux as a KubeArmor backend, but not this exact distro-family wording.

claims.claim_text: KubeArmor enforcement depends on LSM support on the hosts, and some platforms do not enable the required mechanisms out of the box.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. KubeArmor Architecture and Enforcement Boundaries; section: Common Mistakes
claims.rationale: The upstream FAQ directly states that enforcement requires host LSM support and that some environments do not provide it by default.

claims.claim_text: KubeArmor uses an operator/controller plus a node-level daemon, and Relay provides a single gRPC endpoint that aggregates alerts and telemetry.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. KubeArmor Architecture and Enforcement Boundaries; component table
claims.rationale: The official KubeArmor release notes directly describe the operator/controller, daemonset, and Relay roles, but the host is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: KubeArmor has a discovery capability that can generate least-permissive policy drafts from observed workload behavior.
claims.span_hint: section: 3. Designing KubeArmorPolicy From Behavior, Not Syntax
claims.rationale: The official KubeArmor release notes discuss discovery and least-permissive policy generation, but the host is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Falco is primarily a runtime detection and alerting tool rather than a least-privilege inline enforcement layer like KubeArmor.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Choosing KubeArmor Alongside Falco, Tetragon, and Kubernetes Controls; comparison table
claims.rationale: The official Falco docs describe it as a monitoring, detection, and alerting system, but the host is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Tetragon is an eBPF-based security observability system that also provides selected runtime enforcement mechanisms.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Choosing KubeArmor Alongside Falco, Tetragon, and Kubernetes Controls; comparison table
claims.rationale: The official Tetragon docs directly describe eBPF-based observability and runtime enforcement, but the host is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: `karmor logs` supports scoped event viewing with filters such as pod, container, namespace, log type, and operation.
claims.span_hint: section: 4. Rollout Strategy: Visibility, Audit, Enforce, Operate
claims.rationale: The upstream FAQ lists the relevant `karmor log` filtering capabilities directly.

claims.claim_text: A KubeArmorPolicy selector matches pods via labels, so a policy object can apply successfully in the API while protecting no workload if the selector does not match.
claims.span_hint: section: 3. Designing KubeArmorPolicy From Behavior, Not Syntax; Quiz question 7 explanation; Hands-On Exercise step 6
claims.rationale: The upstream policy spec directly documents label-based selectors for KubeArmorPolicy.

further_reading.title: KubeArmor README
further_reading.why_relevant: Best high-level upstream summary of KubeArmor's runtime enforcement model, LSM backends, and telemetry scope.

further_reading.title: KubeArmor Workload Policy Specification
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream reference for selectors, policy sections, and Allow/Audit/Block semantics.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Admission Controllers
further_reading.why_relevant: Clarifies the pre-persistence API boundary that KubeArmor complements rather than replaces.

notes: Code blocks, quiz items, and exercise steps were not turned into standalone claims unless adjacent prose made a factual vendor or Kubernetes behavior assertion. Two backend-table rows were marked cannot_be_salvaged because the exact distro-family wording exceeded what the verified allowlisted sources directly supported.
